 {"id":"xf-33","title":"Epic: Search & Indexing Performance Optimization","description":"## Epic: Search & Indexing Performance Optimization

### Executive Summary
This epic tracks a comprehensive performance overhaul of xf's search and indexing subsystems. The goal is to achieve sub-50ms hybrid search latency and <2 minute indexing for 50K+ document archives while maintaining perfect output isomorphism (identical results before and after optimization).

### Background
Performance profiling revealed five critical bottlenecks in the current implementation:

1. **Embedding Reload per Search (CRITICAL)** - Every search call triggers a full table scan of embeddings (~73MB for 100K docs), rebuilding the VectorIndex from scratch. This adds 500-2000ms latency per search.

2. **N+1 Document Lookups (CRITICAL)** - After vector similarity search returns doc IDs, we fetch each document individually in a loop. For 100 results, this is 100 separate Tantivy queries instead of 1 batch query.

3. **Sequential Embedding Generation (CRITICAL)** - During indexing, embeddings are generated one-at-a-time despite being CPU-bound and embarrassingly parallel.

4. **Stats N+1 Queries (MEDIUM)** - The stats command runs 10+ separate COUNT(*) queries instead of consolidating into a single query.

5. **RRF Fusion Clones (MEDIUM)** - The hybrid search RRF fusion algorithm clones SearchResult objects unnecessarily, creating ~400 heap allocations per search.

### Success Criteria
- [ ] Hybrid search latency: <50ms (currently 500-2000ms)
- [ ] Lexical-only search latency: <20ms
- [ ] Semantic-only search latency: <30ms  
- [ ] Index 50K docs with embeddings: <2 minutes
- [ ] Stats command latency: <100ms
- [ ] Memory usage: <200MB peak for 100K doc index
- [ ] Output isomorphism: All outputs identical to pre-optimization baseline

### Risk Mitigation
- Comprehensive baseline capture before any changes
- Unit tests for each optimization with isomorphism verification
- E2E test script for full regression testing
- Detailed logging for debugging any issues

### Child Features
- xf-35: Embedding caching (P1)
- xf-36: Batch document lookup (P1)
- xf-37: Parallel embedding generation (P1)
- xf-38: Stats query consolidation (P2)
- xf-39: RRF clone elimination (P2)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-12T00:31:47.343553093-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-12T01:07:55.89260838-05:00"}
 {"id":"xf-34","title":"Establish performance baselines before optimization","description":"## Purpose
Establish quantitative performance baselines BEFORE any optimization work begins. These baselines are critical for:
1. Proving optimizations actually improve performance
2. Detecting regressions during development
3. Documenting the before/after impact for each change

## Baseline Measurements Required

### 1. Search Latency Baselines
```bash
# Capture with hyperfine for statistical accuracy
hyperfine --warmup 3 --runs 20 \
  'xf search "rust" --limit 100' \
  --export-json baselines/search_hybrid_100.json

hyperfine --warmup 3 --runs 20 \
  'xf search "rust" --mode lexical --limit 100' \
  --export-json baselines/search_lexical_100.json

hyperfine --warmup 3 --runs 20 \
  'xf search "rust" --mode semantic --limit 100' \
  --export-json baselines/search_semantic_100.json
```

### 2. Index Performance Baselines
```bash
# Full index with embeddings
hyperfine --warmup 1 --runs 5 \
  'xf index tests/fixtures/perf_corpus --force' \
  --export-json baselines/index_full.json
```

### 3. Stats Command Baselines
```bash
hyperfine --warmup 3 --runs 20 \
  'xf stats' \
  --export-json baselines/stats_basic.json

hyperfine --warmup 3 --runs 20 \
  'xf stats --detailed' \
  --export-json baselines/stats_detailed.json
```

### 4. Memory Usage Baselines
```bash
# Peak RSS during search
/usr/bin/time -v xf search 'test' --limit 1000 2>&1 | \
  grep 'Maximum resident set size' > baselines/memory_search.txt

# Peak RSS during indexing
/usr/bin/time -v xf index tests/fixtures/perf_corpus --force 2>&1 | \
  grep 'Maximum resident set size' > baselines/memory_index.txt
```

### 5. Cache Miss Baseline (Critical for xf-35)
```bash
# Measure cold vs warm search latency
# Cold: first search after xf startup
# Warm: subsequent searches in same process
# This establishes the embedding reload overhead we're eliminating
```

## Output File Structure
```
baselines/
├── README.md                 # Baseline methodology documentation
├── commit.txt               # Git commit hash when baselines captured
├── system_info.txt          # CPU, RAM, disk type
├── search_hybrid_100.json   # Hyperfine results
├── search_lexical_100.json
├── search_semantic_100.json
├── index_full.json
├── stats_basic.json
├── stats_detailed.json
├── memory_search.txt
├── memory_index.txt
└── golden_outputs/          # Output snapshots for isomorphism
    ├── search_hybrid_rust.json
    ├── search_lexical_rust.json
    └── stats_basic.txt
```

## Baseline Capture Script
`scripts/capture_baselines.sh`
- Runs all measurements above
- Captures git commit hash
- Records system info (CPU model, RAM, disk type)
- Validates test corpus exists and is correct
- Stores results in baselines/ directory

## Important Notes
- Run on a quiet system (no background processes)
- Use the SAME test corpus that will be used for post-optimization testing
- Capture golden outputs for isomorphism verification
- Document any environmental factors (CPU throttling, etc.)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All baseline measurements captured in baselines/
- [ ] Golden outputs captured for all search modes
- [ ] System info documented
- [ ] Git commit hash recorded
- [ ] Capture script is idempotent (can re-run safely)
- [ ] Results are git-committed for future reference","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-12T00:32:07.003639961-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-12T01:08:44.775815105-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"xf-34","depends_on_id":"xf-47","type":"blocks","created_at":"0001-01-01T00:00:00Z"}]}

+{"id":"xf-35","title":"Feature: Implement embedding caching to eliminate per-search reload","description":"## Problem Statement
Every hybrid or semantic search triggers a full reload of all embeddings from SQLite:

```rust
// src/main.rs:929-949 (current implementation)
let embeddings = storage.load_all_embeddings()?;  // FULL TABLE SCAN every search\!
let mut index = VectorIndex::new(384);
for (doc_id, doc_type, embedding) in embeddings {
    index.add(doc_id, doc_type, embedding);
}
```

### Impact
- **Latency**: Adds 500-2000ms to every search (depending on archive size)
- **Memory**: Creates new allocations for each search, causing GC pressure
- **I/O**: Unnecessary disk reads for unchanged data
- **User Experience**: Unacceptable for interactive use

### Root Cause
The VectorIndex is created as a local variable in cmd_search(), so it's rebuilt from scratch on every invocation. There's no caching or persistence of the loaded embeddings.

## Solution: OnceLock Singleton Pattern

Use Rust's `std::sync::OnceLock` to create a lazily-initialized, thread-safe singleton:

```rust
use std::sync::OnceLock;

static VECTOR_INDEX: OnceLock<VectorIndex> = OnceLock::new();

fn get_or_init_vector_index(storage: &Storage) -> Result<&'static VectorIndex> {
    VECTOR_INDEX.get_or_try_init(|| {
        let embeddings = storage.load_all_embeddings()?;
        let mut index = VectorIndex::new(384);
        for (doc_id, doc_type, embedding) in embeddings {
            index.add(doc_id, doc_type, embedding);
        }
        Ok(index)
    })
}
```

### Why OnceLock?
- **Thread-safe**: Safe for concurrent access (future REPL parallelism)
- **Lazy**: Only loads when first needed
- **Zero-cost after init**: Subsequent accesses are just pointer reads
- **No external dependencies**: Part of std library

## Cache Invalidation Strategy
The cache must be invalidated when:
1. User runs `xf index` (new embeddings generated)
2. Database file is modified externally

Detection approach (see xf-48):
- Store database mtime or version number at cache creation
- Check before each search if stale
- If stale, reload (rare operation)

## Expected Performance Impact
| Metric | Before | After | Improvement |
|--------|--------|-------|-------------|
| First search | 1500ms | 1500ms | 0% (cold start) |
| Subsequent searches | 1500ms | 30ms | 98% reduction |
| Memory per search | +73MB | 0 | No new allocations |

## Child Tasks
- xf-40: Add OnceLock static for VectorIndex
- xf-41: Modify cmd_search to use cached VectorIndex
- xf-48: Add cache invalidation detection
- xf-49: Unit tests for caching behavior","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-12T00:32:47.416514237-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-12T01:13:16.442647204-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"xf-35","depends_on_id":"xf-34","type":"blocks","created_at":"0001-01-01T00:00:00Z"}]}
+{"id":"xf-36","title":"Feature: Batch document lookup to eliminate N+1 in semantic search","description":"## Problem Statement
After semantic search returns document IDs from vector similarity, we fetch full documents one-by-one:

```rust
// src/main.rs:1049-1055 (current implementation)
for hit in semantic_hits {
    if let Ok(Some(result)) = search_engine.get_by_id(&hit.doc_id) {  // N queries!
        results.push(result);
    }
}
```

### Impact
- **Latency**: For 100 results, this is 100 separate Tantivy queries
- **Overhead**: Each query has fixed parsing/planning overhead (~0.5ms)
- **Total cost**: 100 × 0.5ms = 50ms just in query overhead
- **Scalability**: Gets worse as result count increases

### Root Cause
The `get_by_id()` method was designed for single-document lookups (e.g., `xf tweet <id>`). Using it in a loop for batch retrieval is an N+1 anti-pattern.

## Solution: Batch Document Lookup

Add a new `get_by_ids()` method that fetches multiple documents in a single Tantivy query:

```rust
impl SearchEngine {
    /// Fetch multiple documents by ID in a single query.
    /// Returns results in the same order as input IDs (missing IDs omitted).
    pub fn get_by_ids(&self, ids: &[&str]) -> Result<Vec<SearchResult>> {
        if ids.is_empty() {
            return Ok(Vec::new());
        }
        
        // Build a single BooleanQuery with Occur::Should for each ID
        let mut subqueries = Vec::with_capacity(ids.len());
        for id in ids {
            let term = Term::from_field_text(self.id_field, id);
            let term_query = TermQuery::new(term, IndexRecordOption::Basic);
            subqueries.push((Occur::Should, Box::new(term_query) as Box<dyn Query>));
        }
        
        let query = BooleanQuery::new(subqueries);
        let results = self.searcher.search(&query, &TopDocs::with_limit(ids.len()))?;
        
        // Reorder results to match input order
        // ...
    }
}
```

### Why This Works
- Single query plan for all IDs
- Tantivy's posting list intersection is highly optimized
- No repeated schema/searcher acquisition overhead
- Parallel segment scanning within Tantivy

## Expected Performance Impact
| Metric | Before | After | Improvement |
|--------|--------|-------|-------------|
| 100 doc lookup | ~50ms | ~2ms | 96% reduction |
| Query overhead | 100 × 0.5ms | 1 × 0.5ms | 99% reduction |

## Considerations
- Must preserve order of results matching input ID order
- Must handle missing IDs gracefully (document deleted, etc.)
- Should support optional type filtering

+{"id":"xf-37","title":"Feature: Parallelize embedding generation during indexing","description":"## Problem Statement
During indexing, embeddings are generated sequentially despite being CPU-bound and embarrassingly parallel:

```rust
// src/main.rs:781-828 (current implementation)
for chunk in docs.chunks(BATCH_SIZE) {
    for (doc_id, doc_type, text) in chunk {
        let canonical = canonicalize(text);
        let hash = content_hash(&canonical);
        
        // Sequential embedding - wastes multi-core CPUs\!
        match embedder.embed(&canonical) {
            Ok(embedding) => batch.push(...),
            Err(e) => { ... }
        }
    }
}
```

### Impact
- **Indexing time**: 50K docs takes 3+ minutes on 8-core machine
- **CPU utilization**: Only ~12% (1 core of 8)
- **User experience**: Long wait during initial setup

### Root Cause
The code was written sequentially without parallelization. The hash embedder is pure computation with no shared state, making it ideal for parallelization.

## Solution: Rayon Parallel Iterator

Use rayon's `par_iter()` for parallel embedding generation:

```rust
use rayon::prelude::*;

// Parallel embedding generation
let results: Vec<_> = chunk
    .par_iter()
    .map(|(doc_id, doc_type, text)| {
        let canonical = canonicalize(text);
        let hash = content_hash(&canonical);
        
        // Check cache first (thread-safe read)
        if let Some(existing) = storage.get_embedding_by_hash(&hash)? {
            return Ok((doc_id, doc_type, existing, hash, true)); // reused
        }
        
        // Generate embedding (CPU-bound, thread-safe)
        let embedding = embedder.embed(&canonical)?;
        Ok((doc_id, doc_type, embedding, hash, false))
    })
    .collect::<Result<Vec<_>>>()?;
```

### Why Rayon?
- Already a dependency (used in parser.rs)
- Work-stealing scheduler handles load balancing
- Configurable thread pool size
- Zero-overhead abstraction

## Hash Existence Check Optimization
Before generating embeddings, batch-check which content hashes already exist:

```rust
// Pre-check which hashes exist (single query)
let existing_hashes: HashSet<[u8; 32]> = 
    storage.get_existing_hashes(&all_hashes)?;

// Only embed documents with new content
let to_embed: Vec<_> = docs
    .iter()
    .filter(|(_, _, hash)| \!existing_hashes.contains(hash))
    .collect();
```

## Expected Performance Impact
| Metric | Before (8 cores) | After | Improvement |
|--------|------------------|-------|-------------|
| 50K docs indexing | 180s | 30s | 83% reduction |
| CPU utilization | 12% | 90%+ | 7.5x better |
| Embedding throughput | ~280/s | ~1600/s | 6x faster |

## Thread Safety Requirements
- HashEmbedder is stateless and thread-safe
- Storage reads must be thread-safe (SQLite with proper flags)
- Progress bar updates need synchronization

+{"id":"xf-38","title":"Feature: Consolidate stats N+1 queries into single query","description":"## Problem Statement
The `xf stats` command runs 10+ individual COUNT(*) queries:

```rust
// src/main.rs cmd_stats (current implementation)
let tweets = storage.get_tweet_count()?;       // Query 1
let likes = storage.get_like_count()?;         // Query 2
let dms = storage.get_dm_count()?;             // Query 3
let grok = storage.get_grok_count()?;          // Query 4
let followers = storage.get_follower_count()?; // Query 5
let following = storage.get_following_count()?;// Query 6
let blocks = storage.get_block_count()?;       // Query 7
let mutes = storage.get_mute_count()?;         // Query 8
let first_date = storage.get_first_tweet_date()?; // Query 9
let last_date = storage.get_last_tweet_date()?;   // Query 10
// ... more queries for --detailed
```

### Impact
- **Latency**: 10+ round trips to SQLite (~5-10ms each)
- **Total time**: 50-100ms for basic stats
- **Overhead**: Each query has connection/prepare/execute overhead

### Root Cause
Each getter method was written independently for single-purpose use. No batch method exists.

## Solution: Consolidated Query

Create a single method that returns all counts:

```rust
pub struct AllCounts {
    pub tweets: i64,
    pub likes: i64,
    pub dms: i64,
    pub grok_messages: i64,
    pub followers: i64,
    pub following: i64,
    pub blocks: i64,
    pub mutes: i64,
    pub conversations: i64,
    pub first_tweet_date: Option<DateTime<Utc>>,
    pub last_tweet_date: Option<DateTime<Utc>>,
}

impl Storage {
    pub fn get_all_counts(&self) -> Result<AllCounts> {
        // Single query using subqueries or UNION ALL
        let sql = "
            SELECT
                (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM tweets) as tweets,
                (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM likes) as likes,
                (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM direct_messages) as dms,
                (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM grok_messages) as grok,
                (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM followers) as followers,
                (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM following) as following,
                (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM blocks) as blocks,
                (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM mutes) as mutes,
                (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT conversation_id) FROM direct_messages) as conversations,
                (SELECT MIN(created_at) FROM tweets) as first_tweet,
                (SELECT MAX(created_at) FROM tweets) as last_tweet
        ";
        
        let row = self.conn.query_row(sql, [], |row| { ... })?;
        Ok(AllCounts { ... })
    }
}
```

### Why Subqueries?
- Single round trip to SQLite
- SQLite optimizes subquery execution
- All counts computed in one pass through each table
- Atomic snapshot of data (no race conditions between counts)

## Expected Performance Impact
| Metric | Before | After | Improvement |
|--------|--------|-------|-------------|
| Stats latency | 50-100ms | 5-10ms | 90% reduction |
| DB round trips | 10+ | 1 | 90% reduction |

## Backward Compatibility
- Keep individual get_*_count() methods for other callers
- AllCounts struct is additive, not breaking

## Child Tasks

+{"id":"xf-39","title":"Feature: Eliminate unnecessary clones in RRF fusion","description":"## Problem Statement
The RRF fusion algorithm clones SearchResult objects unnecessarily:

```rust
// src/hybrid.rs:129-134 (current implementation)
for (rank, hit) in lexical.iter().enumerate() {
    entry.rrf += 1.0 / (RRF_K + rank as f32 + 1.0);
    entry.lexical_rank = Some(rank);
    lexical_results.insert(hit.id.clone(), hit.clone());  // CLONE\!
    doc_types.insert(hit.id.clone(), hit.result_type.to_string());  // CLONE\!
}
```

And later:
```rust
// src/hybrid.rs:153-154
doc_type: doc_types.get(&doc_id).cloned().unwrap_or_default(),  // CLONE\!
lexical: lexical_results.remove(&doc_id),  // Move, but cloned earlier
```

### Impact
- **Allocations**: ~400 heap allocations per search (100 results × 4 clones)
- **Memory**: Temporary duplication of result data
- **GC pressure**: More allocations = more deallocation work

### Root Cause
The code was written for simplicity, storing cloned data in HashMaps. The FusedHit struct owns its data rather than borrowing.

## Solution: Lifetime-Based Borrowing

Refactor to use references and indices instead of clones:

```rust
pub struct FusedHit<'a> {
    pub doc_id: &'a str,  // Borrow from input
    pub doc_type: &'a str,
    pub score: f32,
    pub lexical: Option<&'a SearchResult>,  // Reference, not owned
    pub in_both: bool,
}

pub fn rrf_fuse<'a>(
    lexical: &'a [SearchResult],
    semantic: &[VectorSearchResult],
    limit: usize,
    offset: usize,
) -> Vec<FusedHit<'a>> {
    // Store indices instead of clones
    let mut lexical_indices: HashMap<&str, usize> = HashMap::new();
    
    for (rank, hit) in lexical.iter().enumerate() {
        lexical_indices.insert(&hit.id, rank);  // &str key, usize value
    }
    
    // Build results using references
    FusedHit {
        doc_id: &hit.id,
        lexical: Some(&lexical[idx]),  // Reference into input slice
        // ...
    }
}
```

### Why Lifetimes?
- Zero-copy: No heap allocations for result data
- Compile-time safety: Rust guarantees references are valid
- Same API ergonomics: Callers don't notice the change

## Complexity Considerations
This is a **medium-complexity refactor**:
- Requires adding lifetime parameters to FusedHit
- Callers must ensure lexical slice outlives FusedHit usage
- May propagate lifetime requirements upstream

## Expected Performance Impact
| Metric | Before | After | Improvement |
|--------|--------|-------|-------------|
| Allocations/search | ~400 | ~50 | 87% reduction |
| RRF fusion time | ~2ms | ~0.5ms | 75% reduction |

## Verification Requirements
- All 9 existing RRF tests must pass unchanged
- Output isomorphism verified (identical results)
- Benchmark before/after with criterion

### New Method Signature
```rust
impl SearchEngine {
    /// Fetch multiple documents by ID in a single query.
    ///
    /// Returns results in the same order as input IDs.
    /// Missing IDs are silently omitted from results.
    ///
    /// # Arguments
    /// * `ids` - Slice of document IDs to fetch
    /// * `doc_type` - Optional filter by document type
    ///
    /// # Performance
    /// Single Tantivy query regardless of ID count.
    /// O(n) where n = number of IDs.
    pub fn get_by_ids(
        &self,
        ids: &[&str],
        doc_type: Option<&str>,
    ) -> Result<Vec<SearchResult>> {
        if ids.is_empty() {
            return Ok(Vec::new());
        }

        // Build BooleanQuery with Occur::Should for each ID
        let mut subqueries: Vec<(Occur, Box<dyn Query>)> = Vec::with_capacity(ids.len());
        
        for id in ids {
            let term = Term::from_field_text(self.id_field, id);
            let term_query = TermQuery::new(term, IndexRecordOption::Basic);
            subqueries.push((Occur::Should, Box::new(term_query)));
        }
        
        let query = BooleanQuery::new(subqueries);
        let top_docs = TopDocs::with_limit(ids.len());
        let results = self.searcher.search(&query, &top_docs)?;
        
        // Build ID -> Result map for ordering
        let mut result_map: HashMap<String, SearchResult> = HashMap::new();
        for (score, doc_addr) in results {
            let doc = self.searcher.doc(doc_addr)?;
            let result = self.doc_to_search_result(&doc, score)?;
            
            // Apply type filter if specified
            if let Some(filter_type) = doc_type {
                if result.result_type.as_str() != filter_type {
                    continue;
                }
            }
            
            result_map.insert(result.id.clone(), result);
        }
        
        // Return in input order
        Ok(ids.iter()
            .filter_map(|id| result_map.remove(*id))
            .collect())
    }
}
```

### Edge Cases to Handle
1. Empty input -> empty output (no query)
2. All IDs missing -> empty output
3. Some IDs missing -> partial results
4. Duplicate IDs -> deduplicated results
5. Type filter excludes all -> empty output

+{"id":"xf-44","title":"Task: Add batch hash existence check to Storage","description":"## Implementation: Add Batch Hash Existence Check

### Purpose
Before generating embeddings, batch-check which content hashes already exist to avoid redundant work.

### Location
`src/storage.rs` - Storage impl

### New Method
```rust
impl Storage {
    /// Check which content hashes already have embeddings stored.
    ///
    /// Returns the subset of input hashes that exist in the embeddings table.
    /// Used to skip re-generating embeddings for unchanged content.
    ///
    /// # Performance
    /// Single SQL query with IN clause, batched if > 900 hashes.
    pub fn get_existing_hashes(&self, hashes: &[[u8; 32]]) -> Result<HashSet<[u8; 32]>> {
        if hashes.is_empty() {
            return Ok(HashSet::new());
        }
        
        let mut existing = HashSet::new();
        
        // Batch to avoid SQLite parameter limits
        for chunk in hashes.chunks(SQLITE_BATCH_SIZE) {
            let placeholders: String = chunk.iter()
                .map(|_| "?")
                .collect::<Vec<_>>()
                .join(",");
            
            let sql = format!(
                "SELECT content_hash FROM embeddings WHERE content_hash IN ({})"
                placeholders
            );
            
            let mut stmt = self.conn.prepare_cached(&sql)?;
            let rows = stmt.query_map(
                rusqlite::params_from_iter(chunk.iter().map(|h| h.as_slice())),
                |row| {
                    let hash: Vec<u8> = row.get(0)?;
                    Ok(hash.try_into().map_err(|_| rusqlite::Error::InvalidQuery)?)
                }
            )?;
            
            for row in rows {
                existing.insert(row?);
            }
        }
        
        Ok(existing)
    }
}
```

### Why Batch Check?
- Computing hash is fast (~1μs)
- Computing embedding is slow (~1ms for hash, ~50ms for ML)
- Batch DB check is O(1) vs O(n) individual checks
- Enables parallel embedding generation (only new content)

### Integration with xf-45

### New Code Structure
```rust
use rayon::prelude::*;

// Step 1: Compute all hashes (parallel, CPU-bound)
let doc_hashes: Vec<([u8; 32], &str, &str, &str)> = docs
    .par_iter()
    .map(|(doc_id, doc_type, text)| {
        let canonical = canonicalize(text);
        let hash = content_hash(&canonical);
        (hash, doc_id.as_str(), doc_type.as_str(), canonical)
    })
    .collect();

// Step 2: Batch check existing hashes (single DB call)
let all_hashes: Vec<[u8; 32]> = doc_hashes.iter().map(|(h, ..)| *h).collect();
let existing = storage.get_existing_hashes(&all_hashes)?;

// Step 3: Filter to only new content
let to_embed: Vec<_> = doc_hashes.iter()
    .filter(|(hash, ..)| !existing.contains(hash))
    .collect();

info!("Generating {} new embeddings ({} cached)", 
      to_embed.len(), 
      doc_hashes.len() - to_embed.len());

// Step 4: Generate embeddings in parallel
let embeddings: Vec<_> = to_embed
    .par_iter()
    .filter_map(|(hash, doc_id, doc_type, canonical)| {
        match embedder.embed(canonical) {
            Ok(emb) => Some((doc_id.to_string(), doc_type.to_string(), emb, Some(*hash))),
            Err(e) => {
                warn!("Failed to embed {}: {}", doc_id, e);
                None
            }
        }
    })
    .collect();

// Step 5: Store batch (sequential, IO-bound)
for chunk in embeddings.chunks(BATCH_SIZE) {
    storage.store_embeddings_batch(chunk)?;
}
```

### Key Design Decisions
1. **Hash computation parallel** - Pure CPU, no shared state
2. **Existence check batched** - Single DB round-trip
3. **Embedding generation parallel** - CPU-bound, thread-safe embedder
4. **Storage sequential** - SQLite handles its own locking

### Thread Safety Requirements
- `HashEmbedder` is stateless (thread-safe)
- `canonicalize()` is pure (thread-safe)
- `content_hash()` is pure (thread-safe)
- `Storage` reads must be thread-safe (SQLite with PRAGMA)

+{"id":"xf-48","title":"Task: Add cache invalidation detection for VectorIndex","description":"## Implementation: Add Cache Invalidation Detection

### Purpose
Detect when the cached VectorIndex is stale and needs reloading. This happens when:
1. User runs `xf index` (new embeddings generated)
2. Database file is modified externally

### Detection Strategy
Compare stored metadata against current database state:

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct CacheMeta {
    /// Database file modification time when cache was created
    db_mtime: std::time::SystemTime,
    /// Number of embeddings when cache was created
    embedding_count: usize,
    /// Schema version of embeddings table
    schema_version: i32,
}

impl CacheMeta {
    fn is_stale(&self, storage: &Storage) -> bool {
        // Check 1: Database file modified?
        if let Ok(meta) = std::fs::metadata(storage.path()) {
            if let Ok(mtime) = meta.modified() {
                if mtime \!= self.db_mtime {
                    return true;
                }
            }
        }
        
        // Check 2: Embedding count changed?
        if let Ok(count) = storage.get_embedding_count() {
            if count \!= self.embedding_count {
                return true;
            }
        }
        
        false
    }
}
```

### Integration with get_or_init_vector_index
```rust
fn get_or_init_vector_index(storage: &Storage) -> Result<&'static VectorIndex> {
    // Check if existing cache is stale
    if let Some(meta) = VECTOR_INDEX_META.get() {
        if meta.is_stale(storage) {
            info\!("VectorIndex cache is stale, will reload on next search");
            // Note: OnceLock can't be reset, so we log and continue
            // The stale cache is better than no cache
            // Full invalidation requires process restart or Lazy<Mutex<Option<T>>>
        }
    }
    
    VECTOR_INDEX.get_or_try_init(|| {
        // ... initialization code ...
    })
}
```

### Limitation: OnceLock Cannot Be Reset
With OnceLock, we cannot force a reload. Options:
1. **Log warning** - User restarts xf or runs new search in new process
2. **Use Lazy<Mutex<Option<T>>>** - More complex, allows reset
3. **Process-per-search** - Shell mode already does this

For CLI usage (most common), this is acceptable since each invocation is a new process.
For shell mode, we may need to add a `/reload` command that restarts the REPL.

### Logging
```
# When cache is stale but still used:
WARN VectorIndex cache may be stale (db modified since load). Run 'xf shell' with '/reload' to refresh.

# When fresh cache is used:
DEBUG VectorIndex cache is fresh (mtime matches)
```

### Testing Requirements
1. Modify database after caching, verify stale detection
2. Verify false positives don't occur (no spurious reloads)
3. Verify shell mode warning is displayed

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CacheMeta struct tracks db_mtime and embedding_count
- [ ] is_stale() correctly detects modifications
- [ ] Warning logged when stale cache detected
- [ ] Shell mode has /reload command (or documented workaround)
- [ ] Tests verify detection logic","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-12T00:49:02.565030129-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-12T01:16:28.129736524-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"xf-48","depends_on_id":"xf-41","type":"blocks","created_at":"0001-01-01T00:00:00Z"}]}

 {"id":"xf-9rf.2","title":"Build architecture map + core workflow understanding","description":"Goal:

","notes":"Architecture map (high level):
- Entry point: src/main.rs wires CLI (Cli/Commands) to command handlers: index/search/stats/tweet/list/export/config/update/completions.
- CLI definitions: src/cli.rs declares subcommands + many flags (types/limit/offset/sort/since/until/replies/context/fields, etc.). Some flags are not yet wired in main.rs (noted for later correctness checks).
- Core modules (src/lib.rs): parser, model, storage (SQLite), search (Tantivy), config/logging/perf/error.

Indexing flow:
1) xf index <archive> (main.rs cmd_index): validate archive/data/, resolve db/index paths, optionally clear existing data.
2) ArchiveParser (parser.rs) parses JS-wrapped JSON (window.YTD.*) with rayon; parse_manifest -> ArchiveInfo; parse_tweets/likes/dms/grok/followers/etc into model types.
3) Storage::open (storage.rs) opens SQLite, sets pragmas, migrates schema, and stores data into normalized tables + FTS5 virtual tables.
4) SearchEngine::open (search.rs) opens/creates Tantivy index; writer adds documents for each data type (id/text/text_prefix/type/created_at/metadata) and commits.

Search flow:
1) xf search <query> (main.rs cmd_search): open SearchEngine and Storage; map DataType -> search::DocType; call SearchEngine::search with limit+offset.
2) SearchEngine::search (search.rs) uses Tantivy QueryParser (text + prefix field) and optional type filter; collects TopDocs; builds SearchResult with highlights (snippet generator) and metadata (stored JSON).
3) main.rs formats results to json/json pretty/csv/compact/text (with highlight -> ANSI conversion in print_result).

Data boundaries:
- Input: archive files under <archive>/data/*.js with JS prefix; parser tolerates whitespace and trailing semicolons.
- Storage: SQLite schema for tweets/likes/dms/grok + FTS5 tables; metadata in archive_info/meta tables.
- Search index: Tantivy index under XF_INDEX; schema fields include id, text, text_prefix (prefix matching), type, created_at, metadata.

Hot paths / correctness-critical:
- parser.rs: parse_js_file, parse_* per datatype; date parsing; numeric parsing; message aggregation.
- search.rs: schema construction, index_* methods, query parsing, snippet generation.
- storage.rs: schema/migrations + store_* insertions and FTS5 updates.
